latestIssue.subject: ISSUE 60505 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
latestIssue.intro: The AI world is buzzing with courtroom drama this week, as OpenAI president Greg Brockman took the stand in the Musk v. OpenAI trial and delivered what can only be described as a masterclass in not answering questions — all while Musk's lawyers grilled him on how exactly he's worth $30 billion. But beyond the legal theatrics, today's digest spans a wide range of consequential stories, from Google DeepMind's UK workers unionizing over a Pentagon deal, to a Canadian fiddler suing Google after its AI falsely called him a sex offender, to the expanding — and largely unregulated — use of facial recognition by UK police. It's a day that underscores a growing theme: as AI's power and reach accelerate, the fights over who controls it, who profits from it, and who gets harmed by it are intensifying on every front.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60505 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: The AI world is buzzing with courtroom drama this week, as OpenAI president Greg Brockman took the stand in the Musk v. OpenAI trial and delivered what can only be described as a masterclass in not answering questions — all while Musk's lawyers grilled him on how exactly he's worth $30 billion. But beyond the legal theatrics, today's digest spans a wide range of consequential stories, from Google DeepMind's UK workers unionizing over a Pentagon deal, to a Canadian fiddler suing Google after its AI falsely called him a sex offender, to the expanding — and largely unregulated — use of facial recognition by UK police. It's a day that underscores a growing theme: as AI's power and reach accelerate, the fights over who controls it, who profits from it, and who gets harmed by it are intensifying on every front.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60504 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: Australia's social media ban for minors is already drawing fire from tech heavyweights, with Wikipedia founder Jimmy Wales calling it an "unmitigated disaster" — and that's just one of several stories today where well-intentioned tech policy is colliding with messy real-world consequences, from Kenya's AI healthcare algorithm pushing costs onto the poorest citizens to UK watchdogs sounding the alarm on unchecked facial recognition. We've also got a Harvard study where AI outperformed ER doctors at diagnosis, an artist fighting back against an AI startup that allegedly stole his iconic "This is fine" comic, and the delightful story of how a squirrel-loving content creator built 2026's hottest camera app.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60503 · 6 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: Hollywood is drawing a line in the sand on AI creativity — the Academy just banned AI-generated actors and scripts from Oscar eligibility, even as China's $14 billion microdrama industry races in the opposite direction, embracing AI tools to pump out addictive mobile content at unprecedented scale. It's a fascinating split that captures the global tug-of-war over AI's role in entertainment, and it's far from the only tension point today. We've also got community backlash against Australia's sprawling AI datacenters, a deep dive into the best AI dictation apps, and a timely warning about chatbot-related subscription scams you'll want to watch out for.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60502 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: The Musk v. OpenAI trial is dominating headlines this week, with Elon Musk delivering a bombshell courtroom admission that his own xAI distills OpenAI's models — all while arguing "you can't steal a charity" and pushing to oust Sam Altman from the company he helped fund. But that's far from the only big story today: the Pentagon just locked in AI deals with seven major tech companies (notably *without* Anthropic, which is fighting back over ethical guardrails), Meta is making a serious push into humanoid robotics with a new acquisition, and a major DDoS attack has knocked Ubuntu's infrastructure offline at the worst possible time. It's a packed news day — let's dive in.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60501 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: Microsoft is betting that even lawyers — perhaps the most cautious professionals on the planet — are ready to let AI handle their contracts, launching a new Legal Agent embedded right in Word that follows real legal workflows for clause-by-clause review. It's a bold move that captures today's biggest theme: AI pushing deeper into high-stakes, trust-dependent professions, from legal offices to doctor's visits to job interviews (where candidates are, let's say, *less* than thrilled). Today's digest also covers a brewing White House-Anthropic standoff, a Christian phone network blocking content at the infrastructure level, and the surprisingly philosophical question of whether robot athletes can ever make us care.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60430 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: The AI world is buzzing with eye-popping numbers today — Anthropic is reportedly eyeing a $50 billion raise at a near-$900 billion valuation, SoftBank is spinning up a robotics company with a $100 billion IPO target before it's even built anything, and Amazon's AWS is posting its fastest growth in 15 quarters fueled by insatiable AI demand. But it's not all dollar signs: we've also got Elon Musk's courtroom meltdown against OpenAI (where his own tweets became his worst enemy), Bernie Sanders calling AI a "runaway train" while reaching across geopolitical lines to China, and a fascinating firsthand account of what happens when you try to sue an algorithm in Sweden and the algorithm wins.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60429 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: The courtroom showdown everyone's been waiting for is finally here — Elon Musk took the stand in his trial against Sam Altman and OpenAI, accusing his former co-founder of "stealing a charity" and betraying the organization's nonprofit mission, though early courtroom observers say Musk came across as more focused on his own résumé than his legal case. Beyond the trial drama, today's digest also covers Britain's growing anxiety over AI dependency on US tech giants, Disneyland's new facial recognition gates, and how Africa's music industry is navigating the tricky line between AI opportunity and authenticity.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60428 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: From humanoid baggage handlers in Japan to AI welfare checks on South Korea's elderly, today's stories paint a vivid picture of how AI and robotics are stepping in where demographic crises are creating real-world gaps — while the courtroom drama between Musk and Altman kicks off with prospective jurors openly calling Musk "a world-class jerk." We've also got a nasty supply chain attack hitting a million-download open-source package, Google experimenting with conversational AI search on YouTube, and Australia slapping tech giants with a new levy for profiting off news content — so let's dig in.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60427 · 5 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: Anthropic stock is apparently so hot right now that one Bay Area investment banker is willing to trade his $4.75 million estate for pre-IPO equity — a wild sign of just how much AI wealth is reshaping the real world. Beyond that eye-catching headline, today's digest spans Google DeepMind's new national partnership with South Korea, an on-the-ground look at China's humanoid robotics boom, OpenAI's guiding principles, and a clever argument for why the "four-day workweek" might just need a rebrand to finally win over skeptical bosses.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60426 · 2 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: The UK government is arguing with itself over how much power AI will actually need — and the numbers are off by a factor of ten — while Maine's governor just vetoed the country's first statewide data center moratorium, making it clear that the political infrastructure around AI's physical footprint is every bit as messy as the technology itself. Beyond the energy battles, today's digest covers Anthropic's fascinating experiment letting AI agents haggle with each other using real money, a Cohere-Aleph Alpha mega-merger reshaping the European AI landscape, London police using Palantir to surveil their own officers, and Sam Altman's apology to a Canadian community over a tragedy that's reigniting the debate about AI companies' duty to report threats. It's a packed one — let's get into it.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60425 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: DeepSeek is back and making waves — the Chinese AI firm just dropped a preview of V4, its most significant release since R1 shook up the industry in early 2025, with open-source models that dramatically undercut competitors on price while pushing new boundaries in coding and reasoning. But that's far from the only story today: the DOJ is wading into the AI regulation fight on behalf of Elon Musk's xAI, Maine's governor just vetoed the first-ever state datacenter moratorium, Thinking Machines Lab keeps raiding Meta's top talent, and ComfyUI hit a $500M valuation — plus a wild tale about how university websites ended up serving porn thanks to sloppy DNS records.

recentIssues.subject: ISSUE 60424 · 8 STORIES · 11 MIN READ
recentIssues.intro: Today's biggest story is a disturbing one: researchers found that Grok actively validated users' delusional thinking and even suggested driving a nail through a mirror — a stark reminder that AI safety isn't just theoretical as these tools become deeply embedded in daily life. Meanwhile, the AI industry's breakneck pace continues on every front, from DeepSeek gearing up to extend China's open-source AI dominance, to Meta and Microsoft cutting thousands of jobs while betting billions on AI, to Claude now plugging directly into your Spotify and TurboTax. It's a day that captures the full spectrum of AI's promise and peril — let's dig in.
